title: The Nervous System and Pottery: How Working With Clay Regulates the Body
excerpt: Discover how working with clay can soothe your nervous system and support trauma recovery through hands-on somatic regulation.
body:
Introduction: The Body-Mind Connection in Healing

Many people come to therapy expecting talk alone to resolve deep-seated trauma or chronic stress, only to find themselves stuck. This is because trauma and dysregulation live in the body as much as the mind. As Bessel van der Kolk highlights in The Body Keeps the Score, healing requires reconnecting with the body, not just the story we tell ourselves.

One accessible, embodied way to engage the nervous system is through pottery — working with clay. This hands-on creative process offers more than just artistic expression; it provides a direct sensory experience that can calm, regulate, and re-pattern the nervous system. In this article, we’ll explore how pottery aligns with somatic healing principles, why it works, and how you can use it as a self-directed tool for nervous system regulation.

Understanding the Nervous System: Polyvagal Theory and Regulation

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory revolutionized how we understand the nervous system’s role in trauma and healing. The autonomic nervous system isn’t just fight, flight, or freeze; it has a social engagement system that supports safety and connection.

Pottery taps into this system by engaging multiple senses and fostering a state of focused attention and calm. When you touch, mold, and shape clay, you activate the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting relaxation and a sense of groundedness.

The Role of Sensory Input in Regulation

Deb Dana, a leading expert on Polyvagal Theory, emphasizes the importance of sensory experiences in shifting nervous system states. Clay provides tactile, proprioceptive, and kinesthetic input — the kinds of sensory information that help the brain understand "I am safe." This sensory grounding is vital for people who experience hypervigilance, anxiety, or dissociation.

Pottery as a Somatic Practice: Body-Based Trauma Recovery

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, teaches that trauma is held in the body’s implicit memory. Healing happens through somatic awareness and gentle movement that helps discharge stuck energy.

Working with clay offers a slow, mindful movement practice that feels safe and controllable. The resistance and malleability of clay invite subtle, intentional actions that increase body awareness and presence.

Engaging Proprioception and Interoception

Pottery requires your hands and body to continually adjust and sense how the clay responds. This engages proprioception — the body’s sense of position and movement — and interoception — awareness of internal states. Both systems are often impaired by trauma, and retraining them can restore a sense of agency and safety, as Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy work suggests.

Creative Flow and Nervous System Co-Regulation

Flow states, described by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, occur when your skills meet the challenge at hand in a focused, enjoyable way. Pottery naturally fosters flow by requiring attention and offering immediate sensory feedback.

This flow state downregulates the stress response, promoting neurochemical shifts that support calm and well-being. Additionally, when pottery is done in a group or with a teacher, the social engagement system activates, further supporting nervous system regulation through co-regulation — a key concept emphasized by Deb Dana.

Emotional Expression and Integration Through Clay

Gabor Maté highlights that trauma is often unrecognized emotional pain that remains trapped in the nervous system. Pottery allows nonverbal expression of feelings that might be too difficult to articulate.

As you shape the clay, you externalize internal states in a tangible form, which can facilitate emotional release and integration. This process complements verbal therapy by accessing different parts of the brain and nervous system.

Mindfulness, Grounding, and Pottery

Dan Siegel teaches the importance of integration — linking different parts of our experience into a coherent whole. Pottery, by its nature, is a mindful practice that grounds you in the present moment.

Focusing on the texture, temperature, and resistance of clay keeps your attention anchored in the here-and-now, which is a powerful antidote to dissociation and rumination. This mindfulness enhances nervous system regulation and emotional resilience.

Getting Started: Practical Tips for Using Pottery as a Self-Directed Practice

  • Find Your Clay: You don’t need a kiln or studio to start. Air-dry clay or modeling clay can provide the tactile experience.
  • Create a Safe Space: Choose a quiet place where you feel comfortable exploring without judgment.
  • Set an Intention: Before you begin, tune into how your body feels and what you might need — relaxation, grounding, expression.
  • Engage Your Senses: Notice the texture, temperature, and resistance of the clay. Move slowly and mindfully.
  • Observe Without Judgment: Allow whatever comes up — emotions, thoughts, sensations — without trying to change them.
  • Reflect: After your session, journal or speak aloud about your experience to integrate insights.
"Healing trauma is not about forgetting what happened, but about learning to live in a body that feels safe and alive." — Bessel van der Kolk

Conclusion: Pottery as a Bridge to Somatic Healing

Pottery offers more than a creative outlet; it’s a somatic tool that helps regulate the nervous system through sensory grounding, mindful movement, and emotional expression. Supported by research from van der Kolk, Levine, Porges, and others, this hands-on practice can complement therapy or stand alone as a self-directed path toward nervous system regulation and trauma recovery.

If you’ve tried talk therapy and still feel disconnected from your body, consider integrating pottery into your healing journey. Your hands, your senses, and your nervous system will thank you.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does working with clay help regulate the nervous system?
faqs.a: Working with clay provides sensory input that activates the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting relaxation and grounding. The tactile and proprioceptive engagement helps shift the body from fight-or-flight to a calm state.

faqs.q: Do I need to be an artist to benefit from pottery in trauma healing?
faqs.a: No artistic skill is required. The benefit lies in the sensory experience and mindful engagement with the clay, not the final product.

faqs.q: Can pottery replace traditional therapy for trauma?
faqs.a: Pottery is a valuable complementary tool for somatic regulation, but it is not a substitute for professional therapy when needed. It can enhance and support healing alongside other treatments.

faqs.q: What if I feel overwhelmed or triggered when working with clay?
faqs.a: Start slowly, focus on your breath, and create a safe environment. If overwhelming emotions arise, pause and practice grounding techniques or reach out to a therapist.
